concepts.title: Standard Results
concepts.conceptBody:
Some useful results

A set of three mutually perpendicular axes $\text{OX},\text{OY},\text{OZ}$ along which unit vectors are respectively $\widetilde{\text{i}},\widetilde{\text{j}},\widetilde{\text{k}}$ constitute a reference frame. If a point $\text{P}$ is represented in its position by the ordered triplet ( $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$) then the vector $\overrightarrow{\text{OP}}=\text{x}\widetilde{\text{i}}+\text{yj}+\text{z}\widetilde{\text{k}}$ represents the position vector of $\text{P}$ with respect to the origin $\text{O}$. Therefore $\text{A}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)$ can be represented uniquely by its position vector drawn from $\text{O}$ to $A$ so that $OA={{x}_{1}}\tilde{i}+{{y}_{1}}\tilde{j}+{{z}_{1}}\tilde{k}$, where $\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}},{{z}_{1}} \right)$ are the coordinates of $A$.

A number of useful results, analogous to what are available in coordinate Geometry, (or in complex number theory) is available in vector also.

  • Thus, if $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{a}$ are the position vector of $A$ and $B$, then the midpoint of A B has position vector $\frac{\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\overrightarrow{\text{b}}}{2}$
  • More generally a point $\text{P}$ dividing the joint of $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ in the ratio $\text{m}:\text{n}$ has position vector $\frac{\text{mb}+\text{n}\vec{a}}{~\text{m}+\text{n}}$.

We also have the following:

If $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are the position vectors of the vertices of a triangle A B C, then

(i) The centroid of the triangle has position vector$\frac{\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\overrightarrow{\text{b}}+\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}{3}$.

(ii) If $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are position vectors of vertices of an equilateral triangle, whose orthocentre is at origin, then $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0}$

(iii) The orthocentre $O$, the triangle has position vector $\frac{(a\sec A)\vec{a}+(b\sec B)\vec{b}+(\operatorname{csec}C)\vec{c}}{a\sec A+b\sec B+c\sec C}$,

(a, b, c are the sides and A, B, C are the angles of the triangle.)

(iv) The circumcentre S of the triangle has position vector $\frac{\sin 2~\text{A}\vec{a}+\sin 2\text{Bb}+\sin 2\text{C}\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}{\sin 2~\text{A}+\sin 2~\text{B}+\sin 2\text{C}}\text{. }$

(v) Further, if $\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}};\overrightarrow{\text{OB}}=\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$,

$\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\text{OB}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}$

Specially if $\vec{a}={{x}_{1}}\tilde{i}+{{y}_{1}}j+{{z}_{1}}\tilde{k}$ so that is $\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}},{{z}_{1}} \right)$ and

$\overrightarrow{\text{b}}={{\text{x}}_{2}}\widetilde{\text{i}}+{{\text{y}}_{2}}\widetilde{\text{j}}+{{\text{z}}_{2}}\widetilde{\text{k}}$ so that $\text{B}$ is $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}},{{\text{z}}_{2}} \right)$ then

$-\text{a}+\overrightarrow{\text{b}}=\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}=\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\widetilde{\text{i}}+\left( {{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\widetilde{\text{j}}+\left( {{\text{z}}_{2}}-{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)\widetilde{\text{k}}$

and hence $|\vec{b}-\vec{a}|=\sqrt{{{\left( {{x}_{2}}-{{x}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{y}_{2}}-{{y}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{z}_{2}}-{{z}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}}$

which gives relatively the distance between $A\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}},{{z}_{1}} \right)$ and $B\left( {{x}_{2}},{{y}_{2}},{{z}_{2}} \right)$.

examples.title:
Consider the following for the next items that follow:
The angles $A, B$ and $C$ of a triangle $A B C$ are in the ratio $3: 5: 4$.
What is the value of $a+b+\sqrt{2} c$ equal to?
If the angle of triangle $A, B$ and $C$ are in $A P$ and $b: a=\sqrt{3}: 1$, then what is the value of $A$ ?
In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$, if $\mathrm{a}=3, \mathrm{~b}=4$ and $\sin \mathrm{A}=\frac{3}{4}$, then $\angle \mathrm{B}$ equals
In a triangle $A B C$, side $\mathrm{C}=2$, angle $\mathrm{A}=45^{\circ}$, side $\mathrm{a}=2 \sqrt{2}$, then what is angle $\mathrm{C}$ equal to?

Consider the following statements:
1. There exists no triangle $A B C$ for which $\sin A+\sin B=\sin C$.
2. If the angles of a triangle are in the ratio $1: 2: 3$, then its sides will be in the ratio $1: \sqrt{3} : 2$. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
